title: Standard deviation of a population
description: Calculate the standard deviation of a population.
topic_path: Variance and standard deviation of a population

assessment_items.question_content:
The following data points are the number of chocolate chips in $4$ different chocolate chip cookies in a package.

>$6, 2, 3,  1$

**Find the standard deviation of the data set.**  
*Round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] chocolate chips
hints:
###How to calculate standard deviation

The formula for standard deviation (SD) is 

>$\Large\text{SD} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum\limits_{}^{}{{\lvert x-\bar{x}\rvert^2}}}{n}}$

where $\sum$ means "sum of", $x$ is a value in the data set, $\bar{x}$ is the mean of the data set, and $n$ is the number of values in the data set.

###Step 1: Finding $\goldD{\bar{x}}$ in $\sqrt{\dfrac{\sum\limits_{}^{}{{\lvert x-\goldD{\bar{x}}\rvert^2}}}{n}}$

$\bar{x}$ is the mean of the data set.

See if you can find $\bar{x}$ yourself. If you're stuck, click *"Explain"* below the problem.

[[☃ graded-group 2]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Step 2: Finding $\goldD{\lvert x - \bar{x} \rvert^2}$ in $\sqrt{\dfrac{\sum\limits_{}^{}{\goldD{{\lvert x-\bar{x}}\rvert^2}}}{n}}$

This is the square of the distance from each data point $x$ to the mean (the deviation).

For example, in the first line of the table below $x = 6$ is $\lvert 6 - \blueD3 \rvert =\redD3$ away from the mean, and $\redD3^2 = 9$. 

[[☃ graded-group 2]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Step 3: Finding $\goldD{\sum\lvert x - \bar{x} \rvert^2}$ in $\sqrt{\dfrac{\goldD{\sum\limits_{}^{}{{\lvert x-\bar{x}}\rvert^2}}}{n}}$

This is the sum $\sum$ of the square of the distances from each of the data points $x$ to the mean $\bar{x}$ (the deviations).

[[☃ graded-group 2]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Step 4: Finding $\goldD{\dfrac{\sum\lvert x - \bar{x} \rvert^2}{n}}$ in $\sqrt{\goldD{\dfrac{\sum\limits_{}^{}{{\lvert x-\bar{x}}\rvert^2}}{n}}}$

This is the sum $\sum$ of the square of the distances from each of the data points $x$ to the mean $\bar{x}$ (the deviations), divided by the number of data points $n$.

[[☃ graded-group 2]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Step 5: Finding  the standard deviation $\sqrt{\dfrac{\sum\limits_{}^{}{{\lvert x-\bar{x}\rvert^2}}}{n}}$

This is the square root of the sum $\sum$ of the square of the distances from each of the data points $x$ to the mean $\bar{x}$ (the deviations), divided by the number of data points $n$. This is it! This is the standard deviation!

[[☃ graded-group 2]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###The answer

**The standard deviation is approximately $1.87$ chocolate chips.**


[[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:

>$41,46,52,49$

This is the square of the distance from each data point $x$ to the mean (the deviation).

For example, in the first line of the table below $x = 41$ is $\lvert 41 - \blueD{47} \rvert =\redD{6}$ away from the mean, and $\redD{6}^2 = 36$.

###Step 3: Finding $\goldD{\sum\lvert x - \bar{x} \rvert^2}$ in $\sqrt{\dfrac{\goldD{\sum\limits_{}^{}{{\lvert x-\bar{x}}\rvert^2}}}{n}}$

This is the sum $\sum$ of the square of the distances from each of the data points $x$ to the mean $\bar{x}$ (the deviations).

###Step 4: Finding $\goldD{\dfrac{\sum\lvert x - \bar{x} \rvert^2}{n}}$ in $\sqrt{\goldD{\dfrac{\sum\limits_{}^{}{{\lvert x-\bar{x}}\rvert^2}}{n}}}$

###Step 5: Finding  the standard deviation $\sqrt{\dfrac{\sum\limits_{}^{}{{\lvert x-\bar{x}\rvert^2}}}{n}}$

###Step 5: Finding  the standard deviation $\sqrt{\dfrac{\sum\limits_{}^{}{{\lvert x-\bar{x}\rvert^2}}}{n}}$

This is the square root of the sum $\sum$ of the square of the distances from each of the data points $x$ to the mean $\bar{x}$ (the deviations), divided by the number of data points $n$. This is it! This is the standard deviation!

[[☃ graded-group 2]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###The answer

**The standard deviation is approximately $2.76$ push-ups.**


[[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
The following data points are last week's revenues (in thousands of dollars) for the $5$ Herman's Hoagies locations. 

>$5,6,5,10,9$

**Find the standard deviation of the data set.**  
*Round your answer to the nearest hundredth.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] thousand dollars
hints:
###How to calculate standard deviation

The formula for standard deviation (SD) is 

>$\Large\text{SD} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum\limits_{}^{}{{\lvert x-\bar{x}\rvert^2}}}{n}}$

where $\sum$ means "sum of", $x$ is a value in the data set, $\bar{x}$ is the mean of the data set, and $n$ is the number of values in the data set.

###Step 1: Finding $\goldD{\bar{x}}$ in $\sqrt{\dfrac{\sum\limits_{}^{}{{\lvert x-\goldD{\bar{x}}\rvert^2}}}{n}}$

$\bar{x}$ is the mean of the data set.

See if you can find $\bar{x}$ yourself. If you're stuck, click *"Explain"* below the problem.

[[☃ graded-group 2]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Step 2: Finding $\goldD{\lvert x - \bar{x} \rvert^2}$ in $\sqrt{\dfrac{\sum\limits_{}^{}{\goldD{{\lvert x-\bar{x}}\rvert^2}}}{n}}$

This is the square of the distance from each data point $x$ to the mean (the deviation).

For example, in the first line of the table below $x = 5$ is $\lvert 5 - \blueD{7} \rvert =\redD{2}$ away from the mean, and $\redD{2}^2 = 4$. 


[[☃ graded-group 2]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Step 3: Finding $\goldD{\sum\lvert x - \bar{x} \rvert^2}$ in $\sqrt{\dfrac{\goldD{\sum\limits_{}^{}{{\lvert x-\bar{x}}\rvert^2}}}{n}}$

This is the sum $\sum$ of the square of the distances from each of the data points $x$ to the mean $\bar{x}$ (the deviations).

[[☃ graded-group 2]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Step 4: Finding $\goldD{\dfrac{\sum\lvert x - \bar{x} \rvert^2}{n}}$ in $\sqrt{\goldD{\dfrac{\sum\limits_{}^{}{{\lvert x-\bar{x}}\rvert^2}}{n}}}$

This is the sum $\sum$ of the square of the distances from each of the data points $x$ to the mean $\bar{x}$ (the deviations), divided by the number of data points $n$.

[[☃ graded-group 2]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Step 5: Finding  the standard deviation $\sqrt{\dfrac{\sum\limits_{}^{}{{\lvert x-\bar{x}\rvert^2}}}{n}}$

This is the square root of the sum $\sum$ of the square of the distances from each of the data points $x$ to the mean $\bar{x}$ (the deviations), divided by the number of data points $n$. This is it! This is the standard deviation!

[[☃ graded-group 2]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###The answer

**The standard deviation is approximately $2.1$ thousand dollars.**
